file_name: Lallas et al. - 2024 - Prediction of melanoma metastasis using dermatoscopy deep features An international multicenter coh.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning and machine learning methods, specifically a pre-trained ResNet 50 network to extract dermatoscopy deep features and a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier, to predict melanoma metastasis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is a poster presentation from an international multicenter cohort study involving original experimental data collection from 712 patients, application of machine learning models (SVM, ResNet 50), and reporting of primary and secondary outcomes (AUC, TPR). It is not a review or summary of prior literature.

title: Prediction of melanoma metastasis using dermatoscopy deep features: An international multicenter cohort study
brief_summary: This international multicenter cohort study investigated whether dermatoscopy deep features can serve as biomarkers for predicting melanoma metastasis. The researchers analyzed data from 712 patients with cutaneous melanoma, utilizing a pre-trained ResNet 50 network to extract deep features and a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier for prediction. The primary outcome compared the prognostic performance of the deep feature model against established histopathologic factors like Breslow thickness and ulceration. The SVM model achieved a mean AUC of 0.84 and a True Positive Rate of 0.81, performing similarly to models using histopathologic factors without statistically significant differences. Additionally, the model correctly classified 91.3% of stage IIB and IIC patients who eventually developed metastasis. The findings suggest that dermatoscopy deep features offer an immediate, in vivo prediction of metastasis prior to excision, potentially guiding neoadjuvant or adjuvant therapy selection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focused on patients with cutaneous melanoma, specifically distinguishing between those that metastasized and those that did not. The cohort included 712 patients: 465 non-metastatic and 247 metastatic.

all_task_types.target: Prediction of melanoma metastasis (metastatic vs. non-metastatic)
all_task_types.details: Binary classification task using a Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier to distinguish between melanomas that metastasized and those that did not, based on dermatoscopy deep features extracted via a pre-trained ResNet 50 network. The study also evaluated classification performance in specific subgroups (stage IIB and IIC patients).

datasets_used.name: International multicenter cohort study dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset from an international, multicenter cohort study of cutaneous melanoma patients from 3 different continents. It includes clinical and dermatoscopic images with adequate follow-up time for the development of metastasis (both locoregional and distant).

use.training: The dataset was separated into training and testing sets, stratified by TNM-stage. This stratified split was repeated five times, resulting in five different training-test sets.
use.testing: The dataset was separated into training and testing sets, stratified by TNM-stage. This stratified split was repeated five times, resulting in five different training-test sets.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the prognostic performance (AUC) of the deep dermatoscopy features model against established prognostic factors and a combined model.
To compare the prognostic performance (AUC) of the deep dermatoscopy features model against established prognostic factors and a combined model.
To estimate the uncertainty of the mean AUC and True Positive Rate (TPR) for the SVM model.
To ensure robustness and guard against biased data selection by creating five different training-test sets stratified by TNM-stage.

hypothesis_testing.test: De Long’s test and ANOVA Kruskal-Wallis
hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no statistically significant difference in the prognostic performance (AUC) between the deep dermatoscopy features model (Model 1) and the established prognostic factors model (Model 2) or the combined model (Model 3).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a statistically significant difference in the prognostic performance (AUC) between the compared models.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No statistically significant differences among models were detected in terms of AUC, as both De Long’s test and ANOVA Kruskal-Wallis yielded p-values greater than 0.05.

models_compared: Model 1: SVM with deep dermatoscopy features
Model 2: Established prognostic factors (Breslow thickness, ulceration)
Model 3: Combined model (deep features + histopathologic factors)

Model 2 (Established prognostic factors).notes: No statistically significant difference compared to Model 1 (De Long’s test p>0.05 and ANOVA Kruskal-Wallis p>0.05)

Model 3 (Combined model).notes: No statistically significant difference compared to Model 1 (De Long’s test p>0.05 and ANOVA Kruskal-Wallis p>0.05)

Model 1 (Stage IIB/IIC subgroup).notes: Patients who eventually developed metastasis during follow-up

International multicenter cohort study.notes: The study found that dermatoscopy deep features could offer an immediate, in vivo prediction of melanoma metastasis prior to excision. No statistically significant differences were detected among models in terms of AUC.

architecture_used.main_model: Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier for prediction; Pre-trained ResNet 50 network for feature extraction
architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet 50 was pre-trained (specific dataset not reported)

training_details.data_split: Stratified by TNM-stage, repeated five times (five different training-test sets)

conclusions: Dermatoscopy deep features can serve as an immediate, in vivo biomarker for predicting melanoma metastasis prior to excision.

The SVM model using deep dermatoscopy features achieved a mean AUC of 0.84 and a True Positive Rate of 0.81, performing similarly to models using established histopathologic factors (Breslow thickness and ulceration) without statistically significant differences.
The model correctly classified 91.3% of stage IIB and IIC patients who eventually developed metastasis during follow-up.
The findings suggest clinical importance in prioritizing high-risk patients for neoadjuvant treatment or guiding the selection of patients who might benefit from adjuvant therapy.
important_remarks: The study is a poster presentation from the American Society of Clinical Oncology (2026), which may imply less rigorous peer review or data availability compared to full journal articles.

The model using only dermatoscopy deep features (AUC 0.84) performed similarly to models using established histopathologic factors (Breslow thickness and ulceration) without statistically significant differences, suggesting limited incremental prognostic value over standard clinical metrics.
The paper does not report the specific dataset on which the pre-trained ResNet 50 was originally trained, which is a potential limitation for reproducibility and understanding feature bias.
Hyperparameters for the Support Vector Machine (SVM) classifier and the specific input image size for the ResNet 50 network are not reported.

The study population is limited to patients with available clinical and dermatoscopic images and adequate follow-up, potentially introducing selection bias regarding disease severity or access to care.

The cohort includes patients from 3 different continents, which supports external validity, but the paper does not report performance metrics stratified by center or geographic region to assess generalizability across diverse populations.

The primary outcome comparison showed no statistically significant difference between the deep learning model and the clinical model (p > 0.05), which may be viewed as a weak evidence for the superiority of the AI approach in this specific context.
